quote: Be the first critical reviewer of your code. Only submit work once it passes self-review.
through_line: Authorship is harder to maintain than it looks. In AI-assisted development, in content built for AI readers, and in maker education for children — the same risk appears: the tool does more, the person does less, and the creative intelligence that was supposed to be supported quietly recedes. Responsibility is not a setting; it is a practice.

articles.title: Practical Use Of AI Coding Tools For The Responsible Developer
articles.pitch: A practitioner's honest account of two years integrating AI coding tools at a professional web agency — Copilot, Cursor, Claude, and ChatGPT. The piece is useful precisely because it doesn't evangelise: AI can hallucinate, the developer must review line by line, and the real gain is cognitive bandwidth for higher-level planning, not a reduction in accountability. A grounded, practice-level take on what AI-assisted development looks like when someone is actually responsible for shipping real work.

articles.title: Your Destination Is Already in AI Search Results. Here's How to Shape What It Says.
articles.pitch: Off Madison Ave's piece focuses on the practical response DMO teams need now: AI is already citing destination content, but citations are shaped by structure, authority, and schema — not design aesthetics. The article moves from diagnosis to tactics: write content that answers questions no one else has answered, structure data for machine readability, and audit what AI actually says about the destination today. Practitioner-level specificity on an urgent problem.

articles.title: Saving Lives By Closing the Intention-Action Gap
articles.pitch: Behavioral Scientist's piece on the intention-behaviour gap — the persistent phenomenon where knowing what to do and actually doing it diverge — examines what structural conditions close that gap rather than just describing it. The research context here is public health, but the mechanism is universal: knowing and doing are governed by different systems, and understanding alone rarely activates the second. A grounded complement to this week's Deep arc on the limits of insight as a change agent.

articles.title: Seeing with Grandmother's Eyes
articles.pitch: Adapted from Margaret Cullen's book Quiet Strength, this Tricycle piece focuses on the near enemies of equanimity — indifference, numbness, emotional flatness — and how to distinguish them from the real thing. The "grandmother's eyes" metaphor frames equanimity as seeing everything with warmth and without preference: not detachment, but full presence without being swept away by what is present. Cullen holds both sides of the shadow-and-clarity theme together without collapsing either into the other.

articles.title: Science in Flux: Is a Revolution Brewing in Evolutionary Theory?
articles.pitch: A 2026 Aeon essay surveying pressure building at the edges of orthodox evolutionary theory — from genomics, epigenetics, and developmental biology. Most evolutionists now agree the field is in flux; some call for an Extended Evolutionary Synthesis that gives developmental processes a causal role they have long been denied. A rich piece for anyone interested in how scientific paradigms resist anomalies until they can no longer afford to, and what happens when the cracks become structural.
